items.prompt: An immutable R × C matrix must answer many axis-aligned rectangle-sum queries. Which state most directly supports constant-time queries after preprocessing?
constraints: Rectangle boundaries may arrive in arbitrary order.
No matrix cell is updated after preprocessing.

options.text: A padded 2D prefix P where P[r][c] stores the sum of rows [0, r) and columns [0, c).
One moving sum for the current row only.
All matrix values sorted into a one-dimensional list.
One minimum value per row.

feedback.reason: A rectangle is bounded independently on two axes, so reusable cumulative state must summarize every row-column boundary pair.
feedback.details: A 2D prefix stores the sum of each top-left half-open rectangle. Any query rectangle is then isolated by inclusion-exclusion of four stored states. One row window, sorted cells, or row minima loses either column boundaries, original geometry, or additive information.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_row_window: A row-only window cannot isolate arbitrary vertical boundaries or combine many rows in constant time.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_cells: Sorting destroys cell coordinates, so a rectangle no longer corresponds to a contiguous geometric region.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.row_minima: Per-row minima are neither additive nor sufficient to reconstruct a rectangle sum.

items.prompt: Under the padded convention, what must P[r][c] mean for the standard four-term rectangle formula to be valid?
constraints: Row 0 and column 0 are zero sentinels.

options.text: The sum of matrix cells with row < r and column < c.
Only the value at matrix cell (r, c).
The sum of whichever rectangle was queried most recently.
The sum of the r × c smallest matrix values.

feedback.reason: Each prefix state must own a fixed top-left half-open rectangle so overlapping regions can be added and removed consistently.
feedback.details: P[r][c] covers rows [0, r) and columns [0, c). This stable geometric invariant lets the query use one bottom-right prefix, subtract two outside strips, and add back their double-subtracted overlap. A dynamic current rectangle or sorted rank has no fixed spatial ownership for inclusion-exclusion.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_cell_value: One cell does not summarize the top-left region, so four such values cannot reconstruct an arbitrary rectangle sum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.current_rectangle_window: A current rectangle changes meaning between queries and provides no reusable state at all boundary pairs.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_rank_sum: Value rank ignores coordinates. Inclusion-exclusion requires geometric containment, not sorted magnitude.

items.prompt: Order the conceptual inclusion-exclusion operations for the inclusive rectangle with corners (r1, c1) and (r2, c2), using padded P.
constraints: P[r][c] sums rows [0, r) and columns [0, c).
Score is based on preserved adjacent relations.

elements.text: Start with P[r2 + 1][c2 + 1], the top-left region through the query’s bottom-right cell.
Subtract P[r1][c2 + 1], the rows above the query.
Subtract P[r2 + 1][c1], the columns left of the query.
Add P[r1][c1], the top-left overlap subtracted twice.
Interpret the remaining contribution as exactly the requested rectangle.

feedback.reason: The two outside strips are removed independently, and their shared overlap must be restored once.
feedback.details: The bottom-right prefix includes the target plus cells above and left of it. Subtracting the top and left prefixes removes those outside regions, but the top-left corner lies in both and is removed twice. Adding P[r1][c1] restores that overlap, completing inclusion-exclusion.

items.prompt: Which formula returns the inclusive rectangle (r1, c1) through (r2, c2) under the padded half-open prefix convention?
constraints: P has a zero sentinel row and column.

feedback.reason: The padded boundaries convert both inclusive lower-right coordinates to +1 and inclusion-exclusion restores the doubly removed top-left overlap.
feedback.details: The first term includes the rectangle plus outside top and left regions. The next two remove those strips, and the last adds back their overlap. Omitting +1 excludes the bottom row or right column, while using only two diagonal prefixes cannot remove the L-shaped outside area.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.omit_overlap_add: The top-left outside region belongs to both subtracted strips, so it would be removed twice without the compensating addition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.use_data_indices_directly: P uses exclusive boundaries. Direct data indices stop before r2 and c2, excluding requested cells.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_term_diagonal: Subtracting one top-left prefix removes only a rectangle, not the separate top and left strips contained in the bottom-right prefix.

items.prompt: Why is one moving rectangular window not equivalent to a 2D prefix for arbitrary rectangle queries?
constraints: Queries can jump between unrelated row and column boundaries.

options.text: A 2D prefix answers from four stored boundary states independent of the previous query, while moving a rectangle may cross many rows and columns.
A window rectangle is mathematically forbidden from changing size.
The 2D prefix is useful because all prefix entries are sorted.
The 2D prefix stores no state, unlike a window.

feedback.reason: Prefix queries are stateless random access over precomputed geometry; a moving rectangle’s update cost depends on how its prior boundaries change.
feedback.details: Four reads and fixed arithmetic isolate any rectangle, regardless of query order. A moving rectangular window can be efficient for a controlled sweep of fixed shape, but arbitrary jumps can require updating many entering and leaving cells. The prefix gains predictable O(1) queries by storing O(RC) cumulative state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rectangles_cannot_move: Moving windows can change position and sometimes size. The issue is the cost and invariant of those updates, not a mathematical prohibition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_values_sorted: Negative cells can make prefix values non-monotonic. Sortedness is unrelated to inclusion-exclusion.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_no_memory: A padded 2D prefix stores (R + 1)(C + 1) entries, which is O(RC) extra space.

items.prompt: Why must matrix cells remain in their original coordinates when building a 2D prefix for rectangle queries?
constraints: Queries identify rectangles by row and column indices.
Cell values may be in any order and may be negative.

options.text: The prefix summarizes geometric top-left regions, so sorting cells would change rectangle membership.
Row sums are valid only when each row is sorted.
A 2D prefix cannot contain negative intermediate sums.
Inclusion-exclusion requires every column to be nondecreasing.

feedback.reason: 2D prefix correctness is spatial, not order-statistical: every state is tied to fixed coordinates.
feedback.details: Addition and inclusion-exclusion work for arbitrary signs and value order. What cannot change is which cell lies inside each top-left region and each query rectangle. Sorting rows, columns, or all cells would answer a different geometric problem.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.addition_needs_sorted_rows: Addition has no sorted-row precondition. The recurrence uses neighboring cumulative regions regardless of value order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative_cells_forbidden: Prefix entries may be negative. Inclusion-exclusion remains an exact algebraic identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.columns_must_be_monotonic: Monotonic columns are irrelevant. The formula relies on containment and cancellation of regions.

items.prompt: Select preprocessing time, one-query time, and extra space for a separate padded 2D prefix over an R × C matrix.
constraints: Each prefix entry is computed in O(1) from neighboring states and one cell.
A query reads four prefix entries.
The input matrix is not counted as extra space.

feedback.reason: Every cell contributes to one prefix entry, each query uses four states, and the padded table stores one state per row-column boundary pair.
feedback.details: The (R + 1) × (C + 1) table is built in O(RC) time and occupies O(RC) extra space. Inclusion-exclusion performs a fixed number of reads and arithmetic operations, so each query is O(1). The constant query time is purchased with a full 2D auxiliary structure; it is not a zero-space prefix method.

items.prompt: Which requirement transfers directly to the same 2D prefix mechanism after changing each cell’s contribution?
constraints: Queries ask about arbitrary axis-aligned rectangles.

options.text: Count obstacle cells in each rectangle by prefixing 1 for an obstacle and 0 otherwise.
Find the median cell value by subtracting four scalar prefix sums.
Change cells online while keeping an unchanged static 2D prefix.
Find the largest rectangle satisfying a constraint using one four-term query only.

feedback.reason: A 0/1 cell contribution remains additive over rectangles, so the same four-term inclusion-exclusion isolates the count.
feedback.details: The geometry and boundary formula stay unchanged; only the per-cell contribution becomes an indicator. A scalar sum does not encode a median, online updates stale many prefix entries, and optimizing over all possible rectangles requires a search or different invariant beyond one region query.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rectangle_median: Four scalar sums retain only total magnitude, not the distribution needed for a median.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_cell_updates: Changing one cell affects every prefix region below and to its right. A static table cannot remain correct without updates or a dynamic structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.largest_valid_rectangle: A four-term query evaluates one supplied rectangle. It does not choose the best rectangle among many candidates.
